options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the graph obtained for Boyle's law is a straight line, not a curved line.

If a chart is plotted between 1/V on the x-axis and the pressure P on the y-axis then a straight line is acquired. This reveals that the pressure and inverse of the volume are directly proportional to each other.
This option is incorrect because the graph obtained for Boyle's law is a straight line, not a zigzag line.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as the correct answer is B) Straight line.

questions.explanation: If a chart is plotted between 1/V on the x-axis and the pressure P on the y-axis then a straight line is acquired. This reveals that the pressure and inverse of the volume are directly proportional to each other.

questions.text: Which of the following has the lowest value of the Van der Waals constant?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: It has nonpolar bonds between its component atoms. This results in a weaker attraction between a nucleus. Moreover, the size of H2 is smaller so fewer London forces are present.
N₂ (nitrogen gas) has stronger intermolecular forces than H₂, so its Van der Waals constant would be higher.
CO₂ (carbon dioxide) has stronger intermolecular forces due to its polar nature and presence of multiple bonds, so its Van der Waals constant would be higher.
Cl₂ (chlorine gas) also has stronger intermolecular forces than H₂, so its Van der Waals constant would be higher.

questions.explanation: It has nonpolar bonds between its component atoms. This results in a weaker attraction between a nucleus. Moreover, the size of H2 is smaller so fewer London forces are present.

questions.text: Which of the following is the deviation of real gases from ideal behavior attributed to?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Boyle's law is an ideal gas law that describes the behavior of ideal gases under different conditions.
Charles's law is an ideal gas law that describes the behavior of ideal gases under different conditions.
Avogadro's law is an ideal gas law that describes the behavior of ideal gases under different conditions.

Van der Waals forces are the weak attractive forces that exist between gas molecules. These forces become important at high pressures and low temperatures, where the gas molecules are closer together and have a greater opportunity to interact. The attractive forces between the gas molecules cause them to stick together more than predicted by the ideal gas law, resulting in deviations from ideal gas behavior.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct since Van der Waals developed an explanation for the deviation of real gas from ideal gas and an equation that was able to fit the behavior of real gases over a much wider range of pressures.

questions.text: The unit for pressure used in engineering work is?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: While the torr is a unit of pressure, it is not commonly used in engineering work. The pascal (Pa), pounds per square inch (psi), and atmospheres (atm) are more commonly used units of pressure in engineering.
The atmosphere (atm) is not commonly used in engineering work. The pascal (Pa), pounds per square inch (psi), is a more commonly used unit of pressure in engineering.

Pounds per square inch (psi) is a unit of pressure that is used to measure the force per unit area exerted by a fluid or gas. It is defined as the pressure resulting from a force of one pound-force applied to an area of one square inch. In engineering work, psi is often used to measure the pressure of fluids and gases in pipes, tanks, and other containers.
The unit Nm-² is not commonly used as a unit of pressure in engineering work. The pascal (Pa), pounds per square inch (psi) is a more commonly used unit of pressure in engineering.

questions.explanation: Pounds per square inch (psi) is a unit of pressure that is used to measure the force per unit area exerted by a fluid or gas. It is defined as the pressure resulting from a force of one pound-force applied to an area of one square inch. In engineering work, psi is often used to measure the pressure of fluids and gases in pipes, tanks, and other containers.

questions.text: Which unit of pressure is commonly used by meteorologists?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Atmospheric pressure is commonly measured with a barometer. In a barometer, a column of mercury in a glass tube rises or falls as the weight of the atmosphere changes.

A pascal (Pa) is the SI (International System of Units) unit of pressure. It is defined as one newton per square meter. The pascal is named after Blaise Pascal, a French mathematician, physicist, and inventor.

The millimetre of mercury is still routinely used in medicine, meteorology, aviation, and many other scientific fields. One millimeter of mercury is approximately 1 Torr, which is 1760 of standard atmospheric pressure ( 101325760 ≈ 133.322368 pascals).
Meteorologists use a metric unit for pressure called a millibar and the average pressure at sea level is 1013.25 millibars.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct since Meteorologists use a metric unit for pressure called a millibar and the average pressure at sea level is 1013.25 millibars. Option A is incorrect since standard atmosphere (atm) is mainly used as a reference value for the average atmospheric pressure at sea level. It is often used to indicate the depth rating for a water-resistant watch but is rarely used as a unit for measuring pressure. Option B is incorrect since the pascal is used to measure sound pressure. Option B is incorrect since even though mm of Hg is not an SI unit, it is still routinely used in medicine, meteorology, aviation, and many other scientific fields.

questions.text: If we decrease temperature of a gas by 2 times, its volume will:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.According to Charles's Law, which describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, if we decrease the temperature of a gas by 2 times (halve the temperature), its volume will decrease by 2 times as well.Therefore, the correct answer is Decrease by 2 times.

This option is incorrect. According to Charles's Law, which describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, if we decrease the temperature of a gas by 2 times (halve the temperature), its volume will decrease by 2 times as well.Therefore, the correct answer is Decrease by 2 times.

According to Charles's Law, which describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, if we decrease the temperature of a gas by 2 times (halve the temperature), its volume will decrease by 2 times as well.Therefore, the correct answer is Decrease by 2 times.

This option is incorrect. According to Charles's Law, which describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, if we decrease the temperature of a gas by 2 times (halve the temperature), its volume will decrease by 2 times as well.Therefore, the correct answer is Decrease by 2 times.

questions.explanation: According to Charles's Law, which describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, if we decrease the temperature of a gas by 2 times (halve the temperature), its volume will decrease by 2 times as well.Therefore, the correct answer is Decrease by 2 times.

questions.text: When P and T are kept constant, V=nRT/P is called _.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: In Boyle's law Temperature and number of moles are kept constant. •V1P1=V2P2 (Boyles law)
In Charles law pressure and no. of moles are kept constant. •V1/T1=V2/T2
In Avagadro law both temperature and pressure are kept constant.
Pascal's law says that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid will be transmitted without a change in magnitude to every point of the fluid and to the walls of the container.

questions.explanation: Avogadro’s Law has pressure and temperature as constant. This is when Volume is directly proportional to the number of moles. This can be combined with Charle’s law and Boyle's law to produce V=RnT/P.

questions.text: If a gas is warmed by 1oC, it will _ by 1/273 of its original volume
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: When a gas is warmed, it generally does not contract. Instead, it tends to expand due to the increased kinetic energy of the gas molecules.
As temperature increases the volume of the gas also increases.
"Shrink" is a term that is often used interchangeably with "contract" or "reduce in size." However, as mentioned before, the general behavior of a gas when warmed is to expand, not to shrink.

This term is not typically used in the context of describing the response of a gas to changes in temperature. When a gas is warmed, it is more accurate to say that it expands rather than being "squeezed."

questions.explanation: As temperature increases the volume of the gas also increases.

This is incorrect as the correct value of R is 8.314Jmol-1K-1
This is incorrect as the correct value of R is 8.314Jmol-1K-1
This is incorrect as the correct value of R is 8.314Jmol-1K-1

questions.explanation: The universal gas constant value is 8.314 Jmol-1K-1.

questions.text: If pressure and volume of a gas are variable while temperature remains constant, this belongs to
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Thermochemistry

options.explanation: This law describes the relationship between the volume and temperature of a gas, assuming pressure is constant. It states that the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.Mathematically:

Avogadro's Law deals with the relationship between the volume of a gas and the number of moles of gas, assuming pressure and temperature are constant. It states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain an equal number of molecules.Mathematically:
Avogadro's Law: V∝n (Volume and amount of gas at constant temperature and pressure).

Pascal's Law is related to fluid mechanics and doesn't specifically apply to gases. It states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to all portions of the fluid and to the walls of its container.

questions.explanation: Boyle's law is a gas law, stating that the pressure and volume of a gas have an inverse relationship. If volume increases, then pressure decreases and vice versa, when the temperature is held constant.

questions.text: For a non-zero value of the force of attraction between gas molecules, the gas equation will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This equation represents the Van der Waals equation, where 'A' accounts for the attractive forces between gas molecules. This modification reflects the reality of molecular interactions in gases, making it suitable for situations where the intermolecular forces are non-zero.

This equation incorrectly adds a term for volume correction but does not account for intermolecular attractions. It does not represent the Van der Waals equation and is thus unsuitable for gases with non-zero attractions.
This equation suggests a correction for volume but does not adequately modify the ideal gas law to account for intermolecular attractions, making it incorrect in the context of the problem.
This is the ideal gas law, which assumes no intermolecular forces. It is not applicable when considering a non-zero force of attraction between gas molecules, hence is not the correct choice.

questions.explanation: The van der Waals equation modifies the ideal gas law for real gases. The term 'a' corrects for the intermolecular forces of attraction, and since these forces are non-zero, the pressure correction term (P + an²/V²) must be included.

questions.text: The compressibility factor Z=PV/RT for 1 mol of a real gas is greater than unity at a pressure of 1 atm and 273.15 K. The molar volume of the gas at STP will be :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. If the compressibility factor Z is greater than 1, it indicates that the gas is behaving less ideally, and the molar volume will be greater, not less, than 22.4L.

This option is correct. A compressibility factor Z greater than 1 suggests that the gas occupies more volume than an ideal gas under the same conditions, so the molar volume at STP will be greater than 22.4L.
This option is incorrect. An ideal gas at STP has a molar volume of 22.4L, but if Z is greater than 1, the real gas's molar volume will be greater than 22.4L.
This option is incorrect because there is a correct answer among the provided options: the molar volume is greater than 22.4L.

questions.explanation: The compressibility factor Z=PV/RT. If Z > 1, it means the real volume is greater than the ideal volume. Since the ideal molar volume at STP is 22.4 L, the molar volume of this gas will be greater than 22.4 L.

questions.text: The root mean square speed of molecules of nitrogen gas is v at a certain temperature. When the temperature is doubled, the molecules dissociate into individual atoms. The new rms speed of the atom is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option calculates the rms speed of N₂ molecules at doubled temperature, but does not account for the dissociation into individual nitrogen atoms, which changes the mass and affects the speed further.

Correct. When temperature doubles, the rms speed of individual atoms increases by a factor of 2, considering both the temperature effect and the fact that each nitrogen atom has half the mass of an N₂ molecule after dissociation.
Incorrect. If the temperature doubles, the rms speed cannot remain the same; it must change due to increased kinetic energy and dissociation.
Incorrect. This option overly exaggerates the impact of temperature doubling and dissociation on rms speed.

questions.explanation: When the temperature of nitrogen gas (N₂) is doubled, the root mean square (rms) speed for the N₂ molecules increases by a factor of √2, due to the formula: v rms = √(3kT/m). However, when the molecules dissociate into individual nitrogen atoms, the mass of each atom is half that of an N₂ molecule. Because the rms speed is inversely proportional to the square root of mass, the speed further increases. Thus, the new rms speed of the nitrogen atoms is 2v. Option A (√2v) only accounts for the temperature change, not the dissociation. Option C (v) suggests no change, which is incorrect due to the temperature increase. Option D (4v) exaggerates the effect of temperature and dissociation.

questions.text: Mean free path of molecules in a rigid container will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Increasing temperature typically increases molecular speed, but in a rigid container, the number density remains constant, so the mean free path does not increase.
While increased temperature might suggest more collisions due to higher speeds, the mean free path does not decrease in a rigid container where density is constant.
In a rigid container, the number density of gas molecules remains constant despite temperature changes, thus the mean free path remains unchanged.
This option is contradictory and not applicable in the context of a rigid container where conditions are uniform. The mean free path remains constant.

questions.explanation: Mean free path is the average distance a molecule travels between collisions. In a rigid container, the volume and number of molecules are fixed, so the particle density (N/V) is constant. Since mean free path depends on particle density, it remains unchanged with temperature.

questions.text: For a real gas obeying van der Waals’ equation, a graph is plotted between PVm (y-axis) and P(x-axis) where Vm is the molar volume. Find the y-intercept of the graph.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The y-intercept of the graph of PVm vs. P for a real gas obeying van der Waals equation is RT. This is derived from the equation when pressure is zero.
This is incorrect. The term a/Vm is related to the correction for intermolecular forces in van der Waals equation and does not represent the y-intercept.
This is incorrect. The term b in van der Waals equation is the correction for finite molecular size and does not affect the y-intercept of the graph.
This is incorrect. The y-intercept can indeed be determined and is equal to RT as shown by the van der Waals equation.

questions.explanation: For a real gas obeying van der Waal’s equation, a graph is plotted between PVm (y-axis) and P (x-axis), where Vm is molar volume. The y-intercept of the graph is RT. This means that when the pressure of the gas is zero, the molar volume of the gas is equal to RT/P, where R is the gas constant and T is the temperature in Kelvin.

questions.text: 0.2 g of a gas X occupies a volume of 0.44 L at the same pressure and temperature. Under identical conditions of P and T, 0.1 g of CO2 gas occupies 0.32 L. Gas X can be :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: O2 has a molar mass of 32 g/mol. The calculated molar mass of gas X is approximately 64 g/mol, so O2 cannot be gas X.
SO2 has a molar mass of 64 g/mol. This matches the calculated molar mass for gas X, making it the correct choice.
NO has a molar mass of 30 g/mol, which is much lower than the calculated molar mass for gas X, so NO cannot be gas X.
C4H10 has a molar mass of 58 g/mol. While closer, it still does not match the calculated molar mass of approximately 64 g/mol for gas X.

questions.explanation: To determine the identity of gas X, we compare the given conditions for CO2 and gas X under the same pressure and temperature. Using the formula:PV = nRT, where n is the number of moles, we can determine the molar mass of gas X.For CO2, 0.1 g occupies 0.32 L. The molar mass of CO2 is 44 g/mol.For gas X, 0.2 g occupies 0.44 L. Using the volume ratios, we find the molar mass of gas X to be approximately 64 g/mol.Therefore, gas X is most likely SO2, which matches the calculated molar mass. Options like O2 and NO have significantly lower molar masses, and C4H10 does not align with the calculated molar mass, confirming that SO2 is the correct answer.

questions.text: Under identical conditions of temperature, the density of gas A is three times that of gas B, while the molecular mass of gas B is twice that of A. The ratio of pressures of A and B will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Explanation is given below.To determine the ratio of the pressures of gases A and B, let's use the ideal gas law equation: PV= NRTThis equation can also be expressed as p=nM/VTherefore, the number of moles n can be expressed as n= PV/MSubstitute n in the ideal gas equation: P= pRT/MNow, let pA and pB represent the densities of gases A and B, respectively, and Ma and Mb their molecular masses. According to the problem:pA = 3pB and Mb + 2MaLet's denote the pressures of gases A and B​. Using the derived pressure formula for each gas:for gas A: Pa = pa Rt/MaFor gas B: Pb =pb Rt/Mbratio of gas A / ratio of Gas Bso pA/pB = 6

This is not correctThis would come from inverting both the density ratio and molar mass ratio , which is not correct because pressure increases when density increases, and also when the molar mass of the same density decreases.

This is not correctThis would come from taking the molar mass ratio but inverting the density ratio, which is physically wrong here — A has a higher density and a lower molar mass, so its pressure must be greater than B’s, not less.
This is not correctThis would come from only taking the density ratio and forgetting the molar mass effect. That ignores the fact that for the same density, a lighter gas exerts more pressure.

questions.explanation: This is the following solution:To determine the ratio of the pressures of gases A and B, let's use the ideal gas law equation: PV NRTThis equation can also be expressed as p=nM/VTherefore, the number of moles n can be expressed as n PV/MSubstitute n in the ideal gas equation: P pRT/MNow, let pA and pB represent the densities of gases A and B, respectively, and Ma and Mb their molecular masses. According to the problem:pA = 3pB and Mb + 2MaLet's denote the pressures of gases A and B​. Using the derived pressure formula for each gas:for gas A: Pa = pa Rt/MaFor gas B: Pb =pb Rt/MbRatio of gas A/ratio of Gas Bso pA/pB = 6
hints: ratio of pressures of A and B

questions.text: The behaviour of a real gas is usually depicted by plotting the compressibility factor Z versus P at a constant temperature. At high temperatures and high pressure, Z is usually more than one. This fact can be explained by the van der Waals equation when:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: The constant ‘a’ is negligible and not ‘b’
options.explanation: Under high temperature and high pressure, the attractive forces between molecules (represented by 'a') become negligible compared to the kinetic energy of the molecules, leading to Z > 1.

options.text: The constant ‘b’ is negligible and not ‘a’
options.explanation: This condition would apply at low pressures where the volume is large, making the volume occupied by gas particles negligible. However, at high pressures, the molecular size (b) is significant.
If both 'a' and 'b' were negligible, the gas would behave ideally under all conditions, which is not the case for real gases at high pressures.

options.text: Both the constants ‘a’ & ‘b’ are not negligible
options.explanation: This situation would not account for Z > 1. If both constants were significant, real gas behavior would deviate significantly from ideal gas behavior under all conditions.

questions.explanation: At high pressures, molecules are close together, so the molecular volume (related to 'b') is no longer negligible. At high temperatures, the kinetic energy is high, making the intermolecular attraction forces (related to 'a') negligible. In this case, the repulsive forces dominate, making the gas less compressible than ideal (Z > 1).

questions.text: Using van der Waals’ equation, calculate the constant, a (atm Ltr2 mole -2) when two moles of a gas confined in a four litre flask exerts a pressure of 11 atm at a temperature of 300 K. The value of ‘b’ is 0.05 Litre mole–1.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct value of 'a' calculated using the Van der Waals equation with the given data.
This option is incorrect as it results from an error in rearranging or substituting values in the Van der Waals equation.
This value is incorrect due to possible mistakes in the calculation process or misinterpretation of the equation components.
This option is incorrect and significantly deviates from the expected value, likely resulting from substantial calculation errors.

questions.explanation: Given:Pressure (P) = 11 atmVolume (V) = 4 LNumber of moles (n) = 2 molesTemperature (T) = 300 KIdeal gas constant (R) = 0.0821 L atm / (mol K)Van der Waals constant 'b' = 0.05 L/molVan der Waals Equation:The equation we'll use is:(P + (an^2 / V^2)) * (V - nb) = nRTSteps:Rearrange the equation to solve for 'a':(P + (an^2 / V^2)) = nRT / (V - nb)(an^2 / V^2) = (nRT / (V - nb)) - Pa = (V^2 / n^2) * ((nRT / (V - nb)) - P)Substitute the given values into the rearranged equation:a = ((4 L)^2 / (2 mol)^2) * (((2 mol * 0.0821 L atm / (mol K) * 300 K) / (4 L - (2 mol * 0.05 L/mol))) - 11 atm)Simplify the equation step by step:a = (16 L^2 / 4 mol^2) * (((49.26 L atm) / (4 L - 0.1 L)) - 11 atm)a = (4 L^2 / mol^2) * ((49.26 L atm / 3.9 L) - 11 atm)a = (4 L^2 / mol^2) * (12.63 atm - 11 atm)a = (4 L^2 / mol^2) * (1.63 atm)Calculate the final value of 'a':a = 6.52 atm L^2 / mol^2

questions.text: A certain volume of a gas at zero degrees Celsius and 1 atm pressure is 0.5 L. If the temperature is raised to 300 degrees Celsius by keeping the pressure constant, then what will be its final volume?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Slightly low; ignores the exact Kelvin ratio.
This volume is too small; it doesn't consider the increase in volume due to the temperature rise.
This is the correct answer. Applying Charles's Law: V1/T1 = V2/T2, V2 = (0.5 L * 573 K) / 273 K = 1.05 L.
This volume is too large, suggesting an exaggerated response to the temperature change.

questions.explanation: Using Charles's Law (V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂), with temperatures in Kelvin. T₁ = 0°C = 273 K, and T₂ = 300°C = 573 K. V₂ = V₁(T₂/T₁) = 0.5 L × (573 K / 273 K) ≈ 1.05 L.

questions.text: At a constant temperature, what should be the percentage increase in pressure for a 5% decrease in the volume of gas:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Pressure increase is not exactly equal to volume decrease, it’s slightly higher.
This option is incorrect. A 10% increase in pressure is too large for a 5% decrease in volume as per Boyle's Law.

This is the correct answer. Boyle's Law states that the product of initial pressure and volume equals the product of final pressure and volume (P1V1 = P2V2). A 5% decrease in volume results in a 5.26% increase in pressure.
This option is incorrect. A 4.26% increase in pressure underestimates the required change calculated using Boyle's Law.

questions.explanation: According to Boyle's Law, P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. A 5% decrease in volume means V₂ = 0.95V₁. Therefore, P₂ = P₁V₁/V₂ = P₁V₁/(0.95V₁) = P₁/0.95 ≈ 1.0526 P₁. This represents a 5.26% increase in pressure.

questions.text: The Kinetic Molecular Theory of gases predicts pressure to rise as the temperature of a gas increases because :
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: The average kinetic energy of the gas molecules decreases
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The Kinetic Molecular Theory states that an increase in temperature results in an increase in the average kinetic energy of the gas molecules, not a decrease.

options.text: Gas molecules collide more frequently with the container walls
options.explanation: Correct! As temperature increases, the average kinetic energy of the gas molecules increases, causing them to move faster and collide with the walls of the container more frequently, which increases the pressure.

options.text: Gas molecules collide less frequently with the container walls
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. An increase in kinetic energy due to higher temperature leads to more frequent collisions with the container walls, not fewer.

options.text: Gas molecules collide less energetically with the container walls
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Higher temperatures increase the kinetic energy of the molecules, resulting in more energetic collisions with the container walls, rather than less energetic ones.

questions.explanation: According to the Kinetic Molecular Theory, as temperature increases, the average kinetic energy of gas molecules also increases. This leads to molecules moving faster, which results in them colliding with the container walls more frequently and with greater force. The increased frequency and energy of collisions contribute to a rise in pressure.Option A is incorrect because it states that the average kinetic energy decreases, which contradicts the theory. Option C suggests that collisions become less frequent, which is also wrong as higher temperature means more collisions. Option D claims that collisions are less energetic, which is false since higher temperatures lead to more energetic collisions.

questions.text: A spherical air bubble is rising from the depth of a lake where the pressure is ‘P’ atm and the temperature is ‘T’ Kelvin. The percentage increase in its radius when it comes to the free surface of the lake will be. (Assume temperature and pressure at the surface are respectively P/4 and 2T Kelvin)
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The change in conditions from the depth of the lake to the surface results in a doubling of the radius, leading to a 100% increase.
This option would imply a smaller increase in radius, which is incorrect given the substantial change in pressure and temperature.
A 40% increase does not account for the doubling of the radius due to the change in conditions, thus it is incorrect.
A 200% increase would suggest the radius triples, which is not supported by the given conditions.

questions.explanation: Ideal Gas Law:The ideal gas law states: PV = nRT, where P is pressure, V is volume, n is the number of moles, R is the ideal gas constant, and T is temperature.Since the number of moles of air in the bubble remains constant, we can write: (P1V1)/T1 = (P2V2)/T22. Given Values:* P1 = P atm (pressure at depth) * T1 = T K (temperature at depth) * P2 = P/4 atm (pressure at surface) * T2 = 2T K (temperature at surface) * V1 = volume of the bubble at depth * V2 = volume of the bubble at surface3. Substituting Values into Ideal Gas Law:** * (PV1)/T = (P/4 * V2) / (2T)4. Simplifying the Equation:** * PV1/T = PV2 / 8T * V1/1 = V2/8 * V2 = 8V15. Volume of a Sphere:** * V = (4/3)πr³, where r is the radius. * V1 = (4/3)πr1³ (volume at depth) * V2 = (4/3)πr2³ (volume at surface)6. Substituting Volume Expressions:** * (4/3)πr2³ = 8 * (4/3)πr1³ * r2³ = 8r1³7. Finding the Relationship between Radii:** * Taking the cube root of both sides: r2 = 2r18. Calculating the Increase in Radius:** * Increase in radius = r2 - r1 = 2r1 - r1 = r19. Calculating the Percentage Increase:** * Percentage increase = (increase in radius / original radius) * 100% * Percentage increase = (r1 / r1) * 100% = 1 * 100% = 100%

questions.text: The system shown in the diagram is at equilibrium at 27oC and the volume of the bulb is 150 mL. At this temperature, the vapour pressure of water is 28 millimetres of mercury. If the bulb contains 0.001 mol of O2(g), the volume of the liquid water is approximately:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is too high since it does not account for the partial pressure of O2 and the vapour pressure of water in the system.
This is the correct answer because it reflects the volume of liquid water that maintains equilibrium with the vapour pressure at the given temperature.
This option is incorrect as it overestimates the volume of liquid water present in the system when considering the equilibria involved.
This option is far too high and does not take into account the contributions of the gas phase to the total pressure in the system.

questions.text: The ratio of the root mean square velocity of H2 at 50 K and that of O2 at 800 K is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This would imply that the velocity of H2 is significantly higher compared to O2 under the given conditions, which is incorrect.
This ratio suggests a moderate increase which does not reflect the correct calculation based on the temperatures and masses of the gases involved.
This ratio indicates that the velocity of H2 is much lower than that of O2. However, based on the calculation, this is incorrect.
This is the correct ratio. It suggests that the root mean square velocities of H2 at 50 K and O2 at 800 K are equal when calculated correctly using the formula.

questions.explanation: Step 1: Write the ratio expressionvrms​(H2​,50K)​/vrms​(O2​,800K) = √TH2.Mo2/MH2.TO2​​Step 2: Substitute values√50 X 0.032 / 0.002 X 800​Step 3: Simplify√1.6/1.6 = 1

questions.text: Vander Waal's equation for CH4 at low pressure is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: At low pressure, Vm_​ is large, so a/V2m (pressure correction) is very small and can be neglected.The volume correction b is still important because it accounts for finite molecular size.Hence, the equation reduces to P(Vm−b)=RTP

This option ignores the volume correction b but keeps the pressure correction a/V2m At low pressure, since Vm is large, a/V2m​ becomes negligible, so it’s incorrect to keep it while ignoring b The volume occupied by gas molecules (b) is always relevant, even at low pressure.

This is the ideal gas law form, which ignores both the pressure correction a and the volume correction b While ideal gas behavior is approached at low pressure and high temperature, the finite volume of methane molecules (represented by b) cannot be ignored completely in Van der Waals equation. So, this is an oversimplification and thus incorrect here.
This formula incorrectly mixes corrections: it subtracts b from pressure P (which is not correct) and subtracts a/V2m​ from volume Vm (also incorrect).

questions.explanation: The Van der Waals equation simplifies by dropping the pressure correction term but keeping the volume correction term, which means:Pressure behaves ideally (no correction needed).Volume available for the gas is reduced by the molecular volume bThis reflects that the gas behaves almost ideally, except that the effective volume is slightly less due to the space occupied by the methane molecules.

questions.text: The rms velocity of hydrogen is √7 times the rms velocity of nitrogen. If T is the temperature of the gas, which of the following is true:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: If the temperatures were equal, the rms velocity of gases with different molar masses would not have the given relationship. This option does not satisfy the given condition that the rms velocity of hydrogen is √7 times that of nitrogen.
This option suggests hydrogen would be at a higher temperature, which would imply a much greater rms velocity than √7 times that of nitrogen, which contradicts the given condition.
This is the correct answer. The relationship between the rms velocities and the molar masses shows that T(H2) must be lower than T(N2) to satisfy the condition vrms(H2) = √7 * vrms(N2).
If this were true, the temperature of hydrogen would need to be √7 times that of nitrogen, which does not align with the given rms velocity relationship involving molar masses and temperature.

questions.explanation: The rms velocity is v = √(3RT/M). Given v(H₂) = √7 v(N₂), we can set up the equation √(3RT(H₂)/M(H₂)) = √7 √(3RT(N₂)/M(N₂)). Squaring and rearranging gives T(H₂)/T(N₂) = 7 × (M(H₂)/M(N₂)) = 7 × (2/28) = 1/2. Thus, T(H₂) is half of T(N₂).

questions.text: The compressibility factor of a gas is less than unity (1) at STP. Therefore,
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This would mean Z>1, which happens when repulsive forces dominate (at high temperature or high pressure), not when Z<1.

Correct. When the compressibility factor (Z) is less than 1, it indicates that the actual volume of the gas is smaller than the ideal gas volume (22.4 L at STP), due to attractive forces among gas molecules.
This is true only for an ideal gas at STP, where Z=1 Our case is real gas with Z<1.
This is incorrect for a compressibility factor less than 1. A value of 44.8 litres would indicate a volume much larger than the ideal gas volume at STP, contradicting Z < 1.

questions.explanation: The compressibility factor Z = V / V . If Z is less than 1, it means the real molar volume (Vm) is less than the ideal molar volume. At STP, the ideal molar volume is 22.4 L, so for this gas, Vm < 22.4 L.

questions.text: Which one is the form of general gas equation?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This is the correct form of the general gas equation, which relates pressure (P), volume (V), number of moles (n), the ideal gas constant (R), and temperature (T). It describes the behavior of an ideal gas under various conditions.

This equation is incorrect as it omits the number of moles (n) and does not represent the general gas equation accurately. It misrepresents the relationship by not accounting for the quantity of gas present.

options.text: P1 V1 / T1 = P2 V2 / T2
options.explanation: This equation represents the combined gas law, which is used to relate the pressure, volume, and temperature of a gas before and after a change, but it is not the general gas equation itself.
This option is incorrect because while the first option is the general gas equation, the other two do not represent it accurately, making this option false.

questions.explanation: The general gas equation, or combined gas law, relates the pressure, volume, and temperature of a fixed amount of gas. It is expressed as PV = nRT.

questions.text: Volume of a gas at STP is 10 dm3. At what temperature will its volume become 30dm3, keeping the pressure constant:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Charles' Law states that volume is directly proportional to temperature in Kelvin, not Celsius. The temperature at which the volume increases to 30 dm3 must be calculated in Kelvin.
This option is incorrect. The calculation should be in Kelvin. While 819K is the correct temperature, converting it directly to Celsius is not appropriate here.
This option is correct. According to Charles' Law, V1/T1 = V2/T2. At STP, T1 is 273K and V1 is 10 dm3. Solving for T2 when V2 is 30 dm3 gives 819K.
This option is incorrect. A temperature of 3K is unrealistically low for a gas volume to expand from 10 dm3 to 30 dm3 under constant pressure.

questions.explanation: Using Charles's Law (V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂), with T₁ being STP (273 K). T₂ = T₁(V₂/V₁) = 273 K × (30 dm³ / 10 dm³) = 273 × 3 = 819 K.

questions.text: Which of the following pairs of gases possesses equal volume at STP:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: 44g of CO2 is 1 mole, while 44g of CO is more than 1 mole due to their different molar masses. Since they do not have the same number of moles, they do not have equal volumes at STP.
16g of O2 is 0.5 moles, while 32g of CH4 is 2 moles. They have different numbers of moles, so their volumes are not equal at STP.

options.text: 3.01×1023 molecules of CO and 3.01 x 1023 gram molecules of H2
options.explanation: 3.01×1023 molecules of CO is 0.5 moles, while 3.01 x 1023 molecules of H2 is also 0.5 moles. However, the term 'gram molecules' is inappropriate and possibly misleading, as it suggests mass rather than quantity of molecules.

options.text: 0.5 mole of NO and 16 g O2
options.explanation: Both 0.5 mole of NO and 16g of O2 (which is 0.5 mole) have the same number of moles. Therefore, they occupy the same volume at STP according to Avogadro's Law.

questions.explanation: According to Avogadro's Law, equal moles of gases at the same temperature and pressure occupy equal volumes. 16 g of O₂ is 0.5 moles. Since both samples contain 0.5 moles, they will have the same volume at STP.

questions.text: Which is incorrect about an ideal gas?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: No force of attraction between molecules
options.explanation: This statement is true for ideal gases. They are assumed to have no intermolecular forces, meaning there's no attraction or repulsion between molecules.
This statement is true. Ideal gases are theoretical constructs that do not exist in nature, as no real gas perfectly adheres to all the assumptions of the ideal gas model under all conditions.

This statement is incorrect. Ideal gases cannot be liquefied easily because they are defined as having no intermolecular forces. Without these forces, gas molecules do not attract each other to form a liquid.

options.text: Obeys gas laws at all temperatures and pressures
options.explanation: This statement is true for ideal gases. They are defined by their adherence to gas laws like Boyle's and Charles's Laws under all conditions, which is not true for real gases.

questions.explanation: Ideal gases cannot be liquefied easily. This is because ideal gases have no intermolecular forces, which are the forces that hold molecules together in a liquid. Without intermolecular forces, the molecules of an ideal gas would simply fly apart when the pressure is increased. An ideal gas cannot be liquefied easily because forces between its molecules are negligible, as a result the molecules do not come closer enough together.

questions.text: A gas initially at 27°C is heated upto 327°C,its average K.E will be:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature (in Kelvin). When the temperature increases from 300K to 600K, the absolute temperature doubles, hence the kinetic energy also doubles.
An increase to four times would require the temperature to quadruple, which is not the case here as it only doubles from 300K to 600K.
An increase of 300 times is incorrect since it implies an unrealistic temperature increase far beyond mere doubling as described in the problem.
This is incorrect as 327 times increase is an arbitrary choice not supported by the temperature change from 300K to 600K.

questions.explanation: The average kinetic energy of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature (in Kelvin). The initial temperature is 27°C = 300 K, and the final temperature is 327°C = 600 K. Since the absolute temperature is doubled, the average kinetic energy will also be doubled.

questions.text: The correct value of the general gas constant R is:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This value is incorrect because the correct value for R in atm dm3 mol-1 K-1 is 0.0821, not 8.314.
This is incorrect because 0.0821 is the value of R in atm dm3 mol-1 K-1, whereas in J mol-1 K-1, R is 8.314.
This is incorrect because the correct value is 62.4 L torr mol-1 K-1, not cm3 mol-1 K-1.
This is the correct value for R when expressed in erg mol-1 K-1, as 1 J = 107 erg.

questions.explanation: The value of the gas constant R depends on the units used. The value 8.314 x 10⁷ erg·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ is a correct, though less common, representation of R. Option A has the wrong units for that value, and options B and C have incorrect values for their units.

questions.text: In gases and liquids, temperature is the measure of_kinetic energies of molecules.
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Temperature measures the average kinetic energy of particles. In gases and liquids, the primary motion is translational, where molecules move freely through space, making translational energy the main contributor to temperature.
Rotational kinetic energy is significant for molecules that can rotate, like diatomic molecules, but it is not the primary contributor to temperature in gases and liquids.

Vibrational kinetic energy involves the movement of atoms within a molecule. It generally becomes significant at higher temperatures and is less relevant for the temperature measurement of gases and liquids.
While rotational and vibrational energies exist, they are not the primary modes of molecular motion that define temperature in gases and liquids. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a system. For gases and liquids, this primarily relates to the translational kinetic energy—the energy associated with the movement of molecules from one place to another

questions.text: The molar volume of nitrogen gas is maximum at:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option provides a moderate temperature; however, the pressure is not minimized to maximize molar volume. Therefore, this is not the correct choice.
At 0°C, the temperature is low, and the pressure is relatively high, resulting in a low T/P ratio. Thus, this option does not provide the maximum molar volume.
This option has the highest temperature and the lowest pressure among the given choices, maximizing the T/P ratio and thus the molar volume. Hence, it is the correct option.
Although the temperature is higher than option A, the pressure is also higher, leading to a lower T/P ratio compared to option C, which does not maximize the molar volume.

questions.explanation: According to the ideal gas law (V = nRT/P), molar volume (V/n) is directly proportional to temperature (T) and inversely proportional to pressure (P). To maximize volume, we need the highest temperature and lowest pressure, which corresponds to 130°C and 1 atm.

questions.text: Gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressure.Which of the following is correct for non-ideality:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.text: At high pressure,the gas molecules move in one direction only
options.explanation: Gas molecules have a random motion,they do not specifically move in one direction.Hence,this option is incorrect.

options.text: At high pressure,the collisions between the gas molecules are increased manifold
options.explanation: At high pressure, the number of collisions between gas molecules does increase,but not necessarily manifold.The pressure of a gas is related to the frequency of collisions,but it does not imply that the collisions increase by a large factor.Hence,this option is incorrect.

options.text: At high pressure, the volume of the gas becomes insignificant
options.explanation: Pressure has an inverse relation with volume.As pressure is increased the volume of gas decreases.But it remains significant as for non-ideal gasses,the volume is significant.Hence,this option is incorrect.

options.text: At high pressure, the inter-molecular attractions become significant
options.explanation: As the pressure of gas increases, the volume of gas decreases. The molecules come close to each other and the forces of attraction between them become significant.Hence,this option is correct.

questions.explanation: At high pressure, gas molecules are crowded, so their finite size and intermolecular attractions cause deviation from ideal behavior. Among these, intermolecular attractions reduce the pressure, making real gases deviate from the ideal gas law

questions.text: Under high pressure, which of the following gases shows more ideality in character:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: Nitrogen (N2) is a diatomic molecule with a moderate molecular mass of 28 amu and weak van der Waals forces, making it behave more ideally under high pressure.
Carbon dioxide (CO2) has a higher molecular mass of 44 amu and stronger intermolecular attractions due to its molecular structure, causing it to deviate from ideal behavior under high pressure.
Ammonia (NH3) has a low molecular mass of 17 amu but forms strong hydrogen bonds, leading to significant deviation from ideal behavior under high pressure.
Sulfur dioxide (SO2) has a high molecular mass of 64 amu and strong polar intermolecular forces, making it far from ideal in behavior under high pressure.

questions.explanation: Gases behave ideally when intermolecular forces are small. N₂ is non-polar with very weak London forces → behaves most ideally. CO₂, NH₃, SO₂ have stronger intermolecular forces (dipole or hydrogen bonding).

questions.text: When 200cm3 of a gas at constant pressure is heated, its volume:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: This option is correct as per Charles's Law, which states that at constant pressure, the volume of a gas increases with an increase in temperature. Heating the gas gives its molecules more energy, causing them to move further apart and thus increasing the volume.
This option is incorrect because according to Charles's Law, the volume of a gas does not remain unchanged when temperature increases at constant pressure. The volume should increase.
This option is incorrect as it contradicts Charles's Law. Generally, a gas's volume will not decrease when heated at constant pressure; it will increase instead.

This option is incorrect for ideal gases under normal conditions. The idea of volume first increasing and then decreasing could apply to real gases under very specific conditions, but not for the scenario described in the question.

questions.explanation: According to Charles law heating a gas at constant pressure increases its volume because the molecules gain kinetic energy and occupy more space.

questions.text: The product of PV of a gas has unit of:
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: States of Matter - Gases, Liquids and Solids

options.explanation: The product of pressure (P) and volume (V) does not correspond to the unit of force. Force is generally measured in newtons (N), which is derived from mass and acceleration, not from pressure and volume.
Entropy is a measure of disorder or randomness in a system and is not directly related to the product of pressure and volume. Its unit is joules per kelvin (J/K), differing from the units of PV.

The product of pressure and volume (PV) is expressed in the same units as work, which is joules (J) in the SI system. This is because work done by a gas during expansion or compression is calculated as the product of pressure and the change in volume.
While enthalpy is related to energy, it is a different thermodynamic quantity that includes internal energy and the product of pressure and volume (PV). However, PV alone does not represent enthalpy.

questions.explanation: The product of pressure and volume (PV) of a gas represents work, which is measured in joules (J). When a gas changes volume under constant pressure, it performs work on its surroundings, calculated as W = P * ΔV, where P is pressure and ΔV is the change in volume. This matches the unit of work. Other options such as force, entropy, and enthalpy involve different physical concepts and units, making them incorrect choices for this question.

questions.text: ﻿The strain of all the difficulties and vexations and anxieties _more than he could bear.
questions.subject: English
questions.topic: Subject Verb Agreement

options.explanation: The subject of the sentence is "The strain", which is a singular noun. Therefore, the correct verb form is "was" to maintain subject-verb agreement.
"were" is a plural past tense verb. It does not agree with the singular subject "The strain" and would incorrectly suggest multiple strains.
"are" is a plural present tense verb. It does not agree with the singular subject "The strain" and also alters the tense of the sentence.
"have been" is a plural present perfect tense verb phrase. It does not agree with the singular subject "The strain" and changes both the tense and aspect of the sentence.

questions.explanation: In this sentence, the main subject is "The strain", which is a singular noun. Therefore, the verb must also be singular to maintain proper subject-verb agreement. The correct answer is "was", as it correctly corresponds to the singular subject.The other options do not fit because "were", "are", and "have been" are all plural forms that do not agree with the singular subject. Using these plural forms would suggest that there are multiple strains, which is not the case in this context.

questions.text: Choose the correct one.
questions.subject: English
questions.topic: Article

options.text: This is cleanest patch I've ever seen,” I bragged.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it is missing the definite article "the" before "cleanest patch." Without the article, the sentence lacks specificity.

options.text: see,” I bragged. This is the cleanest patch I've ever
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as it presents a fragmented sentence. It does not maintain a complete thought, combining two separate ideas without proper structure.

options.text: This is the cleanest patch I've ever seen,” I bragged.
options.explanation: This is the correct option as it properly uses the definite article "the" to specify which patch is being referred to, along with the correct verb form "seen."

options.text: This is a cleanest patch I've ever seen,” I bragged.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it incorrectly uses the indefinite article "a" instead of the definite article "the." The phrase should refer to a specific patch, not just any clean patch.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is option C: This is the cleanest patch I've ever seen,” I bragged.. This option correctly utilizes the definite article "the," indicating that it is a specific patch being discussed, and it correctly employs the past participle "seen." In contrast, option A fails to include the necessary article, making it grammatically incorrect. Option B is a fragmented sentence and lacks coherence, while option D incorrectly uses the article "a" instead of "the," which is not appropriate in this context where a specific instance is being referenced.

questions.text: Spot the error. So, losing no time, the organizers wrote to Michele and me and told ourselves that we had won.
questions.subject: English
questions.topic: Pronoun

options.explanation: "to" is correct as a preposition used before "Michele and me" in the sentence structure.
The phrase "Michele and me" is correct in this context because "Michele and me" are the objects of the verb "wrote to."
"ourselves" is incorrect as it is a reflexive pronoun and is not required in this context. The correct pronoun should be "us."
"had" is correct as it is a past tense verb that agrees with the tense of the sentence.

questions.explanation: "ourselves" is incorrect as it is a reflexive pronoun and is not required in this context. The correct pronoun should be "us."

questions.text: Find the error in following sentence.Over the rocky levels of the mountain was a streak of rust coloured air
questions.subject: English
questions.topic: Punctuations

options.explanation: This prepositional phrase is correctly structured and provides information about the location. There is no error with "Over the rocky levels."
This prepositional phrase is also correctly structured and provides additional information about the location. There is no error with "Of the mountain."
This is the problematic part. When used as a compound adjective to describe "air," it should be hyphenated as "rust-colored." This ensures clarity and correctness in modifying the noun.
This is correctly used as the noun being described. So there is no error in this option

questions.explanation: This is the problematic part. When used as a compound adjective to describe "air," it should be hyphenated as "rust-colored." This ensures clarity and correctness in modifying the nounThe correct answer is C) Rust coloured because the phrase should be hyphenated when used as a compound adjective. Hyphenate "rust coloured" to "rust-colored."Corrected Sentence:"Over the rocky levels of the mountain was a streak of rust-colored air."This correction ensures the compound adjective is correctly hyphenated, providing clear and accurate modification of the noun "air."

questions.text: Select the most suitable synonym for the given word:CONFRONT
questions.subject: English
questions.topic: Passages

options.explanation: Avoid means to stay away from or evade something.

Provoke means to stir up or incite something, often deliberately"Confront" means to face someone or something directly, often with a sense of challenge or opposition, which is similar to "provoke". While "confront" can also mean to face a problem or issue head-on, "provoke" is the closest match among the options.
Solace means to comfort or console someone.
Support means to give assistance or backing to someone.

questions.explanation: Confront is to approach someone face to face, or stand up to them. Provoke is to elicit a reaction or draw forth someone (by confronting them).

questions.text: Statements: Good voice is a natural gift but one has to keep practicing to improve and excel well in the field of music.Conclusions:I. Natural gifts need nurturing and care.II. Even though your voice is not good, one can keep practicing.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.topic: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: This is correct. The statement provides a specific example (a good voice) of a 'natural gift' that requires 'practicing' (nurturing) to 'improve and excel'. Conclusion I is a logical generalization of this idea. Conclusion II is not supported as the statement does not discuss what one should do if their voice is not good.

This is incorrect. The statement's scope is limited to people with a 'good voice'. It offers no information or advice for those whose voice is not good, so Conclusion II cannot be logically derived from the given text.

This option is incorrect. For an 'either/or' choice to be considered, both conclusions usually need to be plausible under different interpretations. Here, Conclusion I clearly follows, while Conclusion II clearly does not, making this option invalid.
This is incorrect because Conclusion I is a valid inference. The statement explicitly links a 'natural gift' (good voice) with the need for practice, which is a form of 'nurturing and care'.

questions.explanation: The statement directly links a natural gift (good voice) with the need for practice (nurturing) to excel. This strongly supports Conclusion I. The statement only discusses what to do with a "good voice" and does not provide any information about what one should do if their voice is not good.
hints: natural gift but one has to keep practicing

questions.text: Find the missing number in the series: 1, 5, 13, 25, ?
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.topic: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Correct. The differences between consecutive numbers increase by 4: 4, 8, 12, 16. Adding 16 to 25 results in 41.
Incorrect. This assumes a difference of 19 after 25, which breaks the established pattern of increasing differences by 4.
Incorrect. This choice does not follow the pattern of increasing differences. The difference after 25 should be 16, not a decrease.
Incorrect. Choosing this option disregards the increasing nature of the differences, as the number should be greater than 25.

questions.explanation: The difference between consecutive terms increases by 4 each time. The differences are +4, +8, +12. The next difference will be +16. Therefore, the missing term is 25 + 16 = 41.

questions.text: Statements: 1. Joe is younger than Kathy. 2. Mark was born after Joe. 3. Kathy is older than Mark. If the first two statements are true, the third statement is:
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.topic: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Given that Joe is younger than Kathy and Mark was born after Joe, it logically follows that Kathy is older than Mark.
This option suggests a contradiction to the logical deduction that Kathy is older than Mark, which is not the case given the first two statements.
There is sufficient information in the first two statements to determine that Kathy is older than Mark, making this option incorrect.
Option A correctly deduces the relationship, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: If Joe is younger than Kathy, then Kathy is older than Joe. If Mark was born after Joe, then Mark is also younger than Joe. Therefore, Kathy is older than Joe, who is older than Mark, which means Kathy must be older than Mark.

questions.text: Statements: I. The university authorities have instructed all colleges under its jurisdiction to ban the use of cell phones inside the college premises. II. Majority of the teachers of the college signed a joint petition to the university complaining the disturbances caused by cell phone ring tones inside the classrooms.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.topic: Logical Reasoning

options.text: Statement I is the cause and statement II is its effect
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the instruction from the university authority is a response to a complaint, not the cause of it.

options.text: Statement II is the cause and statement I is its effect
options.explanation: The complaint made by the teachers about disturbances caused by cell phones led the university authority to instruct colleges to ban cell phones.

options.text: Both the statements I and II are independent causes
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as the statements are related; one is the cause and the other is the effect, not independent causes.

options.text: Both the statements I and II are effects of independent causes
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because both statements are not effects of separate causes; rather, one is a cause and the other is its effect.

questions.explanation: The petition and complaints from the teachers (Statement II) provided the reason and impetus for the university authorities to take action. The resulting ban on cell phones (Statement I) is the effect of these complaints.

questions.text: Statement: The Chairman stressed the need for making education system more flexible and regretted that the curriculum has not been revised in keeping with the pace of the changes taking place.Courses of Action:I. The curriculum should be reviewed and revised periodically.II. The system of education should be made more flexible.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning
questions.topic: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.
Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.
Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.
Clearly, the situation demands making the education system more flexible and changing it periodically according to the needs of the time. So, both the courses follow.

questions.explanation: The statement explicitly identifies two problems: a need for flexibility and an outdated curriculum. Therefore, both courses of action directly address the issues raised by the Chairman and are logical follow-ups.
